TV Remote Control

Control Your TV from Your Phone

A universal smart TV remote app that turns any smartphone into a full-featured remote — supporting all major TV brands with intuitive touchpad navigation, media controls, and a clean dark interface built for the living room.

Role
UI/UX Designer
Timeline
2 Weeks
Platform
Mobile (iOS/Android)
Category
Utility & Entertainment
Tools
Figma

Project Overview

TV remote controls get lost. They run out of batteries. They sit across the room when you need them most. For most households, the physical remote is a constant source of minor frustration — and yet the alternatives (phone apps) have historically been cluttered, unreliable, or ugly.

TV Remote Control was designed to be the remote app that actually replaces the physical one — familiar enough that any family member can pick it up and use it instantly, but built natively for a touchscreen so it feels better than the plastic version it replaces. The result is a clean, dark-mode-first remote interface that works with all major Smart TV brands right out of the box.

The Problem

Through competitive analysis of existing TV remote apps and user research around living room habits, I identified the core pain points:

  • Lost or Broken Remotes: Users frequently need a remote when the physical one isn't available — the app needs to be a reliable, always-on replacement
  • Poor Dark Mode Design: TV remotes are used in dark rooms — most competitor apps are light-themed and hurt the eyes in low light
  • Cluttered Layouts: Existing remote apps cram every button onto one screen, making it hard to find and tap the right control quickly
  • Limited Compatibility: Most remote apps only support a single TV brand, forcing users to maintain multiple apps for different devices
  • No Touchpad Feel: Physical remotes have tactile feedback — touchscreen remote apps rarely replicate the feel of smooth directional navigation

Target Audience

Primary Users: Everyday Smart TV owners (ages 18–60) who want a reliable backup remote on their phone — or who simply prefer the convenience of controlling their TV without reaching for a separate device.

Secondary Users: Tech-savvy users with multiple Smart TVs across different brands who want a single universal remote app that handles all their devices.

Design Process

1. Research & Competitive Analysis

I analyzed the top TV remote apps on the Play Store and App Store — including Mi Remote, AnyMote, and Peel Smart Remote — evaluating their layout hierarchies, button groupings, dark mode implementations, and overall usability in a low-light couch scenario.

The defining insight: users operate TV remotes without looking at them. Physical remotes work because their buttons have spatial memory — your thumb knows where Volume is without your eyes leaving the screen. A digital remote must replicate this spatial familiarity as closely as possible.

2. Layout Hierarchy

I organized the remote controls by usage frequency and interaction type:

  • Zone 1 — Navigation: The D-pad with OK at center — the most-used control, placed prominently at the top with large touch targets
  • Zone 2 — Quick Actions: Return, Exit, Mute, and Power — high-frequency single-tap actions placed directly above and beside the D-pad
  • Zone 3 — Media Controls: Volume, Channel, Zoom, Settings, and playback controls — grouped logically in the middle section
  • Zone 4 — Number Pad: The traditional numeric keypad at the bottom for channel entry — familiar layout matching physical remotes

3. The Home Screen

The main remote interface is designed around one principle: make the most important controls the biggest and most accessible. The D-pad dominates the upper portion of the screen — its large size makes directional taps accurate and forgiving, a critical consideration for living room use where users may be tired, distracted, or operating at arm's length. Every button has generous padding to prevent mis-taps.

4. Dark-First Design Philosophy

The entire app is designed dark-first — a pure black (#111) background with dark grey buttons and blue accents. This is intentional: TV remote apps are used predominantly in dimly lit living rooms and bedrooms. A bright white interface would be jarring and impractical. The dark theme isn't aesthetic; it's functional.

Key Features

Feature 1 — Instant TV Control

The app connects to Smart TVs via Wi-Fi and IR, giving users instant control without complicated setup. The home screen is the remote — there's no dashboard or onboarding to get past. Open the app, and you're controlling your TV.

Feature 2 — Smooth Touchpad Navigation

The D-pad is the centerpiece of the remote. Designed with large, finger-friendly segments, it supports directional navigation (Up, Down, Left, Right) and OK confirmation — covering 90% of everyday TV interaction. The circular form mirrors physical remote D-pads, leveraging existing muscle memory rather than asking users to relearn navigation.

Feature 3 — Universal Smart TV Compatibility

The app works with all major Smart TV brands — Samsung, LG, Sony, TCL, Haier, Toshiba, Roku, Android TV, and Sense — making it a single solution for households with multiple televisions or users who have switched brands. This broad compatibility is a primary differentiator and a core part of the app's marketing proposition, communicated clearly in the Play Store screenshots.

Feature 4 — Full Media & Channel Controls

Beyond navigation, the remote includes Volume +/−, Channel +/−, zoom in/out, settings, and full media playback controls (rewind, play/pause, fast-forward) — giving users complete control without needing to supplement with the physical remote for any function.

Design Decisions

Color Palette

Every color in the interface serves a functional purpose — not just an aesthetic one:

#111111
Base — Dark Background
#3B82F6
Blue — D-pad & Primary
#F59E0B
Orange — Return Button
#10B981
Green — Exit Button
#EF4444
Red — Power Button

Color as function: Return (orange), Exit (green), and Power (red) each have distinct colors that match universal remote conventions — users instantly recognize these buttons without reading the labels. The blue D-pad stands out as the primary interaction zone. This color coding makes the remote usable even in low-light conditions where fine text is hard to read.

Touch Target Sizing

All interactive buttons exceed the minimum 44×44pt touch target guideline, with the D-pad segments designed significantly larger to accommodate the precision required for directional navigation. The number pad uses generous padding between keys to eliminate accidental mis-taps during casual channel browsing.

Play Store Screenshot Strategy

The three Play Store screenshots were designed as marketing assets rather than simple screen captures. Each one pairs a bold headline with a lifestyle photo and the remote interface — communicating the app's value proposition (instant control, smooth navigation, universal compatibility) in a format that converts browsers into downloads.

Challenges & Solutions

Challenge 1

Problem: Fitting all necessary remote functions on a single screen without making buttons too small to tap accurately

Solution 1

Organized controls into four distinct zones by usage frequency — navigation at top with large targets, quick actions beside it, media controls in the middle, and the number pad at the bottom — matching the layout logic of physical remotes

Challenge 2

Problem: Designing for low-light living room environments where screen brightness and contrast are critical

Solution 2

Built dark-first throughout — pure black background, dark grey button surfaces, and high-contrast white labels — ensuring the app is comfortable to use in a dark room without any eye strain

Challenge 3

Problem: Making buttons distinguishable without looking, replicating the spatial memory of physical remotes

Solution 3

Used distinct colors for Return, Exit, and Power — universally recognizable remote conventions — combined with consistent spatial positioning so users can find critical buttons by location rather than label

Challenge 4

Problem: Communicating broad TV brand compatibility in the Play Store without making the screenshot feel like a logo wall

Solution 4

Designed the compatibility screenshot with brand logos overlaid on a dynamic streaming content background — reinforcing that the app works with the content and brands users already know and use

Design Principles

Familiarity First

The layout mirrors physical remote conventions so users need zero learning curve — pick it up and it just works

Dark Room Optimized

Every design decision — background, contrast, button size — was evaluated against a low-light couch scenario

Color = Function

Each color in the interface is assigned a purpose, not an aesthetic role — making critical buttons identifiable even without reading labels

Zero Friction Access

No onboarding, no setup screens — the remote is the first screen the user sees when they open the app

Key Takeaways

  • Context defines design: Designing for a dark living room fundamentally changes color, contrast, and button sizing decisions — environment-first thinking produces better utility apps
  • Familiarity is a feature: For a tool users already know (a TV remote), the best UX closely mirrors what they're used to rather than reinventing the interaction model
  • Color coding replaces labels: In low-light or fast-glance scenarios, color is processed faster than text — using conventional remote button colors (red for power, green for exit) reduces cognitive load
  • Store screenshots are UX too: Designing marketing assets as part of the project ensures the visual story told in the store matches the actual product experience

Impact & Results

This project demonstrates my ability to tackle utility UX where the primary challenge is familiarity, accessibility, and environment-specific design — rather than visual novelty.

  • Designed a complete universal TV remote interface optimized for low-light, living room use
  • Established a four-zone control hierarchy matching physical remote spatial conventions
  • Used color-coded buttons to replicate physical remote muscle memory on a flat touchscreen
  • Created three Play Store marketing screenshots designed to communicate value and drive downloads
  • Built a dark-first design system with a five-color functional palette (black, blue, orange, green, red)

Next Steps

If this project were to move forward, I would:

  • Design a TV brand selection screen allowing users to pair the app with their specific TV model for optimized control
  • Add a gesture touchpad mode as an alternative to the D-pad — letting users swipe to navigate the TV cursor like a laptop trackpad
  • Design a keyboard screen for Smart TV text input — a critical missing feature in most remote apps when searching for content
  • Explore a widget version of the most-used controls (Volume, Power, D-pad) accessible from the phone's home screen without opening the app
  • Conduct usability testing in actual low-light conditions to validate button sizing and contrast choices under real usage scenarios

Thanks for reading!
